summary: The key clinical point is that new-onset signs like increased blood pressure, proteinuria, or thrombocytopenia specifically signal superimposed preeclampsia in women with chronic hypertension. In contrast, fresh retinal changes—though sometimes observed—are not part of official diagnostic criteria because their appearance can result from longstanding hypertension alone; thus, absence of change does not rule out disease, nor do existing ones uniquely confirm it. This makes them less reliable compared to other parameters which have clearer temporality and definition within recognized standards.
